Hollow straws thinner than hair.<[silence:900ms]> Stronger than steel by weight.<[silence:900ms]>

The name carbon nanotube describes its shape:<[silence:120ms]> tubes of pure carbon, rolled like microscopic sheets of chicken wire.<[silence:900ms]> Each wall is a single layer of atoms—imagine a soda straw shrunk 50,000 times, yet able to carry currents better than copper.<[silence:900ms]>

They form when carbon vapor condenses onto metal particles, self-assembling into cylinders.<[silence:900ms]> Some grow as straight as arrows;<[silence:100ms]> others spiral like staircase railings.<[silence:900ms]> A bundle the width of a human hair could suspend a car.<[silence:900ms]> In labs, they’ve been spun into fibers tougher than Kevlar, or used as scaffolding to regrow nerve cells.<[silence:900ms]>

Their weirdest trick?<[silence:150ms]> Conducting heat along their length while staying cool sideways—like a frying pan that only burns in one direction.<[silence:900ms]>

Three related subjects are graphene’s flat cousin, buckyballs’ soccer-ball shapes, and how spiders inspired synthetic silk.<[silence:900ms]>
clean_story:
Hollow straws thinner than hair. Stronger than steel by weight.

The name carbon nanotube describes its shape: tubes of pure carbon, rolled like microscopic sheets of chicken wire. Each wall is a single layer of atoms—imagine a soda straw shrunk 50,000 times, yet able to carry currents better than copper.

They form when carbon vapor condenses onto metal particles, self-assembling into cylinders. Some grow as straight as arrows; others spiral like staircase railings. A bundle the width of a human hair could suspend a car. In labs, they’ve been spun into fibers tougher than Kevlar, or used as scaffolding to regrow nerve cells.

Their weirdest trick? Conducting heat along their length while staying cool sideways—like a frying pan that only burns in one direction.

Three related subjects are graphene’s flat cousin, buckyballs’ soccer-ball shapes, and how spiders inspired synthetic silk.
